doc: Capitation reweighting public page
status: Sourced and read from public page
key: Capitation was introduced in 2002; is core funding for general practice; current formula based on late-1990s use; new formula considers age, sex, multimorbidity, rurality and deprivation; expected from 1 July 2026 subject to PHO Services Agreement negotiation.
implication: Supports background; also shows current reform is allocation-oriented and still capitation-centred.

doc: Cabinet paper: Primary Health Care Funding Improvements
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: States NZ already has a blended model of capitation, co-payments and targeted streams; capitation totalled $915.7m in 2024/25; structure is complicated; poor visibility of what funding buys; reweighting based on >2m patients across 18 PHOs.
implication: Requires package to acknowledge the Government’s own description of a blended model and poor funding visibility.

doc: Briefing H2025066443: Reweighting capitation funding in primary care
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: The Ministry recommended Cabinet agreement for adding variables; says technical specification of formula is operational for Health NZ; proposed variables include expanded age bands, deprivation, geography, multimorbidity and ethnicity, with some content redacted.
implication: Supports the view that formula details remain partly operational/opaque and policy-visible variables are only part of the issue.

doc: Briefing H2025068320: Capitation reweighting – further advice
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Reweighting based on updated workforce and utilisation data from over 2m patients across 18 PHOs; uses available data and assumptions to estimate costs; multivariate regression estimates impact of characteristics as predictors of primary care costs.
implication: Strengthens technical background and supports ‘formula debates are assumption-heavy’ framing while avoiding overstatement.

doc: Briefing H2024057558: Sapere report and re-weighting primary care capitation funding
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Confirms reweighting work is a technical analysis to improve the formula and does not consider fundamental changes to the overall primary care funding model.
implication: Central citation for why broader architecture remains in scope.

doc: Cabinet material: Transforming primary care – first steps / next steps / delivering timely care
status: Sourced and read from public release page and snippets
key: Public release includes Primary Care Tactical Action Plan, first steps, next steps, digital-first enrolment, after-hours care, strategic narrative, workforce appendix, Sapere briefing, letter to PHOs and related minutes.
implication: Current reform comparator must be broader than capitation reweighting alone.

doc: Briefing H2025061978: Developing a health target for primary care
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Advises on new target; first primary care access target; focuses on general practice; accountability linked to capitation; scope/measure choices include redactions; target intended to increase accountability and assurance of investment.
implication: Supports KPI salience game and highlights target is narrower than whole primary/urgent/ambulance access.

doc: Primary care health target public page
key: Proposed target: more than 80% of people can access an appointment with a general practice provider within one week; effective from 1 July 2026.
implication: Used for target background and KPI discussion.

doc: National Primary Care Dataset public page and guide
key: Dataset will collect general practice encounter and appointment information; includes booked date, seen date, outcome; data flows from April 2026; used for target reporting from July 2026; public reporting by end-2026.
implication: Supports data observability game and model validation roadmap.

doc: National Primary Care Dataset key sector themes and detailed scenarios
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Flags unintended consequences of access target, including booking behaviour, planned care, follow-up, chronic care, rural patients, elderly patients and transport/family-support needs.
implication: Adds balancing measures and gaming risk to KPI game.

doc: Primary Care Tactical Action Plan
key: Describes >$640m primary/community investment; 80% access target; national dataset; performance-based funding; capitation formula update; online 24/7 GP care; fewer closed books; slower ED growth; workforce aims.
implication: Current reform comparator must include target, data, digital care, workforce and funding increases.

doc: Vote Health Estimates 2025/26
key: Separate appropriations exist for Delivering Hospital and Specialist Services and Delivering Primary, Community, Public and Population Health Services.
implication: Corrects earlier residual-budget framing: formal separation exists; the game is about salience, transfers, baselines and operational pressure, not an unrestricted single pot.

doc: Supplementary Estimates 2024/25 Vote Health
key: Shows 2024/25 supplementary appropriations and confirms separate primary/community/public/population and hospital/specialist scopes.
implication: Supports corrected budget architecture discussion.

doc: DPMC/Treasury release on allocation/commissioning budgets
status: Sourced and read from search-rendered PDF text
key: Cabinet agreed in principle to condensed Vote Health appropriations, including separate primary/community/public/population, hospital/specialist and hauora Māori appropriations.
implication: Further corrects budget framing and supports discussion of why separate appropriations were designed.

doc: OECD programme budgeting in New Zealand health
key: OECD describes NZ’s new programme structure as designed to rebalance away from hospital dominance and toward primary/community/public health, while acknowledging separate programmes can hinder integration.
implication: Adds nuance: the reform design already recognised the hospital dominance game.

doc: Health and Disability System Review final report / Section C
key: Recommends Tier 1 funding ringfencing in medium term, locality approach, no requirement to contract through national PHO Services Agreement, and multiple contracting options for general practice.
implication: Supports place-based accountability and PHO function/intermediation distinction.

doc: Commissioning for Pae Ora Healthy Futures 2022
key: Framework grounded in Te Tiriti o Waitangi and whānau-centred commissioning; supports commissioning as relational, place-aware and equity-focused.
implication: Place-based accountability and Te Tiriti legitimacy must be explicit guardrails.

doc: PHO finances: a summary of available information H2025069314
key: Ministry lacks direct access to PHO financial activity and ownership structures; PHOs vary widely and do not follow uniform accounting; monitoring/regulation constrained; work follows previous PHO roles/functions/options advice H2025067082.
implication: PHO critique should focus on transparency, pass-through and accountability rather than blanket abolition.

doc: Aide-mémoire H2025070512: Meeting with General Practice New Zealand
key: GPNZ represents 25 of 30 PHOs covering ~4.4m people; Ministry provided advice on PHO challenges and future remit/function H2025067082; Minister emphasised accountability via outcome framework and performance measures over function changes.
implication: Adds PHO accountability game and OIA target.

doc: May 2025 advice list showing H2025067082 and H2025067086 withheld
key: Lists H2025067082 and H2025067086 as sent 30 May 2025, title withheld under s9(2)(f)(iv).
implication: Transparency/OIA section should say existence is public, content is not.

doc: Primary care enrolment districts H2025070691
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Enrolment enables capitation and accountability; enrolment is the only way to access capitation-subsidised care; providers may restrict or close enrolments; practices weigh extra capitation against quality of care, staff availability and wellbeing.
implication: Very directly supports capitation marginal-supply/closed-books mechanism.

doc: Health NZ PHOs by region
key: Health NZ contracts PHOs to provide primary health services; PHOs manage contracts to general practices; most people are enrolled with a general practice and PHO; page lists PHOs by region.
implication: Supports description of PHO role and current architecture.

doc: Tend PHO approval announcement
key: Tend approved as its own PHO; claims direct contracting with Te Whatu Ora; ~80,000 enrolled patients initially; calls current system outdated and supports direct contracting.
implication: Shows market-entry/corporate PHO dynamics and direct-contracting narrative.

doc: RNZ: GenPro breakaway PHO approval and corporate PHOs
key: Health NZ conditionally approved GenPro/thePHO; RNZ notes approvals for Green Cross in November and Tend in May; GenPro EOI from >800,000 patients; GPNZ says GPs already get 100% of per-person funding.
implication: Adds market-entry and pass-through nuance; requires careful use as news not official data.

doc: GenPro final approval for thePHO
key: GenPro says Te Whatu Ora final approval confirms thePHO; frames model as reducing bureaucracy and directing funding to frontline care; thePHO operationally independent from GenPro.
implication: Supports contested PHO intermediation narrative but with stakeholder-interest caveat.

doc: Urgent and after-hours healthcare programme
key: Aims for 98% of New Zealanders to access urgent care within one hour’s drive; workforce package; full range of health professionals; framework developed by Health NZ and ACC; rollout over two years.
implication: Urgent care must be treated as a core upstream access game.

doc: Beehive urgent and after-hours healthcare announcement
key: Budget 2025 invests $164m over four years; 98% in-person urgent care within one hour; new 24h urgent care services and rural/remote improvements.
implication: Current reform comparator includes urgent care, not just GP capitation.

doc: New and improved urgent and after-hours healthcare factsheet / overview
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Up to 80 new/improved after-hours and urgent services; rural communities to benefit from 24/7 on-call clinicians, diagnostics and urgent medicines.
implication: Supports urgent care as hospital-deflection infrastructure; adds scope/workforce and rural place dimensions.

doc: ACC provider payments: Paying you for your services
key: ACC pays under Cost of Treatment Regulations, contracts or purchase orders; payment requires clinical suitability, quality, right time/place, necessary treatment, qualified provider, documentation and pre-approval where required; general practitioners, nurses and nurse practitioners receive consultation and procedure contributions.
implication: Core analogy for uncapped but rule-controlled FFS stream.

doc: ACC health sector engagement / commissioning changes
key: ACC says it is moving toward a collaborative co-commissioning model, with providers, suppliers, clinical leadership, outcome data and transparency.
implication: Updates ACC section from funder-only to commissioner orientation.

doc: MBIE review of ACC Cost of Treatment Regulations
key: Biennial review objectives include affordability for claimants, ACC cost sustainability/consistency and avoiding payment differences across health and ACC systems.
implication: Supports co-payment/equity and fiscal governance framing.

doc: MBIE options for ACC payment increases
key: Cost of Treatment Regulations cap ACC’s amount per treatment; claimants may pay co-payments; rising provider costs can increase co-payments and affect access.
implication: Helps explain ‘uncapped activity but capped item prices’ and copayment risks.

doc: ACC Working Together handbook
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: Explains ACC expectations for Cost of Treatment Regulation providers; ACC pays specified contributions for listed treatment providers within scope of practice.
implication: Supports rule-controlled FFS and scope-based provider eligibility.

doc: Public Health Acute Services Annual Service Agreement briefing H2025065722
status: Sourced and read from public release/search-rendered PDF text
key: ACC payment to Crown for 2025/26 PHAS is $1.053754b; methodology applies Health NZ costs with a two-year data lag and cost-pressure uplift.
implication: Supports ACC/Health NZ cross-funder interaction and hospital/acute cost linkage.

doc: PBFF media analysis NZMJ
key: Analysed 487 newspaper articles; PBFF significant and subject to little independent oversight/public scrutiny; formula attracted discontent and transparency concerns, especially South Island coverage.
implication: Adds formula-fixation caution and transparency precedent.

doc: PBFF 2015 Technical Report
key: PBFF equitably distributes bulk of DHB funding; incorporates population, age, socioeconomic status, ethnicity, sex, and adjustors; review recommended no structural changes but changes to rural adjuster.
implication: Adds analogy: formula reweighting can improve allocation but not necessarily fix architecture or behaviour.

doc: New Zealand Health Survey 2023/24
key: One in four adults reported appointment wait too long; one in six adults reported cost barrier to GP; GP visits down over five years while ED visits up for adults and children.
implication: Supports access constraint and hospital-flow hypothesis.

doc: New Zealand Health Survey 2024/25
status: Sourced and read for availability; not used heavily
key: Updated survey exists with data collected July 2024-July 2025; some 2023/24 corrections were identified and integrated into the Annual Data Explorer.
implication: Future updates should use 2024/25 explorer for final numeric claims where relevant.

doc: Closed books survey NZMJ 2024
key: 2022 survey of 227 practices: only 28% freely enrolled new people; 79% had closed or limited enrolments since 2019; equity/access concerns.
implication: Supports market-entry/access constraint and capitation supply game.

doc: Area-based closed books description
key: 347 general practices (33%) had closed books in June 2022; geography varied materially.
implication: Supports spatial/place-based access analysis.

doc: RACMA Board-agreed P&A priority foci
status: Not sourced: internal/private document referred to in email
key: Gillian indicated Danielle would send RACMA policy and advocacy priorities; not present in supplied trail and not publicly sourced.
implication: RACMA pathway must be framed conditional on fit with priorities; cannot claim alignment until document reviewed.

doc: Cabinet decision shifting hospital appropriation funding to primary care for enhanced capitation
status: Not publicly verified; email intelligence only
key: Debbie stated Cabinet shifted significant money from hospital service appropriation to primary care to cover additional enhanced capitation; I have not located a public decision matching this exact claim.
implication: Mention only as unverified stakeholder intelligence/OIA target, not fact.

doc: Urgent-care advice said nationwide face-to-face availability was not evidence-based
status: Not publicly verified; email intelligence only
key: Debbie indicated current government stance was not advice provided based on evidence; public material shows urgent-care policy but not contrary evidence advice.
implication: Use as OIA target/sensitivity issue, not factual assertion.

doc: Full PHO roles/functions/options advice H2025067082
key: Existence confirmed through H2025069314/H2025070512 and May advice list; title/content withheld under s9(2)(f)(iv).
implication: Transparency/OIA target; avoid claiming contents.

doc: Document labelled ‘Official’ in Dylan anecdote
status: Not sourced in this work; user anecdote only
key: Email anecdote about MoH/HNZ denying existence of a document labelled Official; not in material available to me.
implication: Can be used, if at all, as personal anecdote in voice; not part of evidence base.
